subsystem: ELF note parser from file(1)/libmagic. This is the donote() routine in src/readelf.c, which walks the NOTE segments/sections of an ELF image and decodes vendor note headers (GNU version, GNU Build-ID, NetBSD/FreeBSD/OpenBSD/DragonFly version tags) and, under ELFCORE, core-dump notes (NetBSD PROCINFO, generic PRPSINFO) to produce the human-readable strings `file` prints for an ELF object.
provenance: Identified by the libmagic-specific symbols and idioms: struct magic_set *ms and file_printf(ms, ...) (the libmagic output sink), the Elf32_Nhdr/Elf64_Nhdr note-header types, the class-selecting xnh_* accessor macros (xnh_addr/xnh_sizeof/xnh_namesz/xnh_descsz/xnh_type), ELF_ALIGN, elf_getu32(swap, ...), the NT_GNU_VERSION/NT_GNU_BUILD_ID/NT_NETBSD_VERSION/NT_FREEBSD_VERSION/NT_OPENBSD_VERSION/NT_DRAGONFLY_VERSION/NT_NETBSD_CORE_PROCINFO/NT_PRPSINFO note-type constants, prpsoffsets()/NOFFSETS, os_style_names[], and the FLAGS_DID_* bitmask. The sample banner `primevul_c_train_3082_fixed` marks it as a PrimeVul C sample. This is the classic file/readelf.c donote function.
trust_boundary: The entire input is attacker-controlled: `vbuf`/`nbuf` is a buffer mapped from an ELF file supplied to file(1) (a tool routinely run on untrusted uploads by web services, mail scanners, and archivers), and `size` is the amount of trusted data in that buffer. `offset`, the note contents, and above all the note header's namesz/descsz length fields and the name/descriptor bytes are all taken verbatim from the file. Every array index derived from namesz, descsz, doff, noff, or the fixed core-dump offsets (0x7c, 0x08, prpsoffsets()) is therefore hostile and must be bounded against `size` before the byte is touched.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Signature of donote(). `ms` is the libmagic handle used only as the destination for file_printf() output. `vbuf`/`size` describe the in-memory ELF image and the number of valid bytes in it. `offset` is the cursor into that image at which the current note header begins. `clazz` selects 32- vs 64-bit note layout (it drives the xnh_* macros), `swap` says whether multi-byte fields need byte-swapping, `align` is the note padding granularity, and `flags` is an in/out bitmask (FLAGS_DID_NOTE, FLAGS_DID_BUILD_ID, FLAGS_DID_CORE, FLAGS_IS_CORE, ...) that records which note kinds have already been emitted so the caller's loop does not print them twice. The function returns the next offset to resume parsing at.

Local state. `nh32`/`nh64` are stack copies of the fixed-size ELF note header for the two classes; the xnh_* macros expand to whichever one `clazz` selects. `noff` will hold the offset of the note NAME, `doff` the offset of the note DESCRIPTOR (payload). `os_style` (core builds only) records which OS convention a core note follows and stays -1 until recognised. `namesz`/`descsz` are the attacker-supplied name and descriptor byte-lengths pulled from the header. `nbuf` re-casts the void buffer to an unsigned char* so byte offsets can be indexed.

The fixed part of the note header is copied out of the buffer at `nbuf[offset]` into the local nh32/nh64 (xnh_addr is &nh32 or &nh64, xnh_sizeof is sizeof(Elf32_Nhdr) or sizeof(Elf64_Nhdr)), then `offset` is advanced past it. This is the first read of the buffer and it is done with NO local check that `offset + xnh_sizeof <= size` — the routine trusts the caller's loop to have validated that a full note header fits.

namesz/descsz are read from the (possibly byte-swapped) header via the xnh_* accessors. A note whose name and descriptor are both zero-length is treated as the terminator: the function stops, returning `offset` if it already ran to/past the end, otherwise `size` so the caller stops looping. Both values are 32-bit and fully attacker-controlled.

Sanity gate on the two length fields. It rejects only values with the top bit set (>= 0x80000000), i.e. it treats namesz/descsz as if a negative 'signed' size were the only illegal case. Any value up to 0x7FFFFFFF (nearly 2 GiB) passes this filter, which is the only upper bound applied to the raw lengths before they are used in the offset arithmetic below.

The name starts at the current offset (noff). The descriptor offset doff is computed as offset+namesz rounded up to the alignment via ELF_ALIGN. The guard `offset + namesz > size` then rejects notes whose name would run off the end of the buffer, returning doff so the caller advances. This bounds the NAME region [noff, noff+namesz) to lie within `size`, but note that doff is the ALIGNED value, which can be a few bytes larger than offset+namesz.

The next resume offset is doff+descsz aligned up, and the guard `doff + descsz > size` rejects notes whose descriptor would run past the end, returning the appropriate stop value. After this point the code assumes the DESCRIPTOR region [doff, doff+descsz) is fully inside the buffer. Crucially, this guarantees only `descsz` bytes at doff — it says nothing about reads at fixed sub-offsets (0x08, 0x7c, prpsoffsets()) that may exceed descsz.

If both a version note and a Build-ID have already been emitted, the vendor-note decoding below is skipped and control jumps straight to the `core:` label to try core-dump note handling. This is a once-guard so the same descriptive strings are not appended repeatedly across many notes.

The GNU OS-version note handler. It only fires when name is exactly "GNU", type is NT_GNU_VERSION, and descsz is exactly 16. It copies 16 bytes (sizeof(desc), desc being uint32_t[4]) from the descriptor into a stack array and then switches on desc[0] to print the OS name (Linux/Hurd/Solaris/kFreeBSD/kNetBSD/<unknown>) followed by desc[1..3] as a version triple. Because descsz==16 exactly matches sizeof(desc) and doff+descsz<=size was verified, this copy is in-bounds.

The GNU Build-ID handler. Name "GNU", type NT_GNU_BUILD_ID, descsz constrained to 16 or 20. It copies descsz bytes into desc (uint32_t[5] = 20 bytes), so the copy never exceeds the destination, and prints descsz>>2 words as hex. Sets FLAGS_DID_BUILD_ID. Note there is NO `return` here, so after appending the Build-ID execution continues to the following handlers and eventually to core handling.

NetBSD version note: name "NetBSD" (namesz 7), type NT_NETBSD_VERSION, descsz==4. It copies 4 bytes into a single uint32_t, byte-swaps it, and decodes it as a packed __NetBSD_Version__ (major/minor/release/patch) using the arithmetic on lines 133-153. Fixed 4-byte descriptor within the verified descsz==4 region — the copy is in-bounds; the rest is integer formatting.

FreeBSD version note: name "FreeBSD" (namesz 8), type NT_FREEBSD_VERSION, descsz==4. Copies a 4-byte __FreeBSD_version into desc, swaps it, and runs the long branch of pure integer arithmetic (lines 193-232) to print the release string. Only in-bounds descriptor reads and arithmetic; no buffer indexing beyond the verified 4 bytes.

OpenBSD (name "OpenBSD", descsz==4) prints a constant string and returns; it does not read the descriptor at all. The DragonFly handler that follows (lines 246-258, name "DragonFly" namesz 10, descsz==4) copies the 4-byte descriptor, swaps it, and prints a three-part version. Both are gated on exact namesz/descsz and read only in-bounds descriptor bytes.

The `core:` section classifies the note owner by matching the name against "CORE" (SVR4), "FreeBSD", or "NetBSD-CORE", setting os_style to a valid enum index. These matches use the name bytes at noff, which were bounded by the earlier offset+namesz<=size guard; os_style remains -1 if nothing matches so os_style_names[] is only indexed with a valid value later.

Core-note handling (only compiled with ELFCORE). If a core note has already been emitted it returns immediately. Otherwise, if a style was recognised and not yet printed, it appends e.g. ", netbsd-style" using os_style_names[os_style]; os_style is guaranteed to be a valid enum value here because it is only set from the fixed matches above.

NetBSD core PROCINFO decode. It prints the program name from a fixed sub-offset 0x7c inside the descriptor (up to 31 chars via %.31s) and reads a 4-byte signal number from fixed sub-offset 0x08. Both reads use doff plus a hardcoded offset with NO check that descsz (or size) actually extends that far — the only guarantee in scope is descsz bytes at doff, and descsz for a PROCINFO note is fully attacker-controlled.

Generic PRPSINFO command-name extraction. Because the program name lives at a different offset per OS, it tries each candidate offset in prpsoffsets(0..NOFFSETS-1). For each, an inner loop scans up to 16 bytes and, importantly, DOES bound each access with two guards: noffset>=size (physical buffer end) and reloffset>=descsz (logical descriptor end), bailing to tryanother if either is exceeded. So the per-character reads at nbuf[noffset] on line 365 are properly bounded.

A candidate offset is accepted if the 16-byte window is printable and either NUL-terminates (after j>0) or is fully printable. On success the code then runs a secondary loop (lines 396-407) that, for each later candidate k whose offset is smaller, walks nbuf[no] from doff+prpsoffsets(k) up to doff+prpsoffsets(i) checking isprint(), to pick the best (earliest) match. That secondary scan reads nbuf[no] using ONLY the prps offsets, without the noffset>=size / reloffset>=descsz guards that protected the inner j-loop.

Finally it forms cname at doff+prpsoffsets(i) and re-scans it with `for (cp = cname; *cp && isprint(*cp); cp++)` to find the end of the printable command name, trims trailing spaces, and prints the range. This final scan is bounded only by encountering a NUL or a non-printable byte — it has no `cp < nbuf+size` limit, so it relies entirely on such a byte existing before the buffer ends. Line 431-433 (`return offset;`) is the fall-through return when nothing matched.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: For a NetBSD NT_NETBSD_CORE_PROCINFO note the code reads the program name at the hardcoded sub-offset doff+0x7c and lets %.31s scan up to 31 bytes. The only length guarantee in scope is that descsz bytes exist at doff (from the line-50 check). An attacker can craft a PROCINFO note whose descsz is small (e.g. 4) so that doff+0x7c already lies at or beyond doff+descsz and, if the note sits near the end of the mapped image, beyond `size`. %.31s then reads out of bounds (up to 31 bytes past the valid region, and further because %.Ns stops at a NUL, which may not appear), leaking adjacent memory into the printed output or crashing. Invariant: before reading at doff+0x7c the code must verify descsz >= 0x7c + 32 (and doff+0x7c+31 < size). CWE-125.

Same PROCINFO path: it memcpy's 4 bytes from the fixed sub-offset doff+0x08 with no check that descsz >= 0x0c. If the attacker sets descsz < 12 and the note is near the buffer end, this reads up to 4 bytes past the descriptor / past `size`. It is a smaller over-read than the name case but has the same root cause: fixed core-dump offsets applied to a variable-length, attacker-sized descriptor. Invariant: verify descsz >= 0x08 + sizeof(signo) before the copy. CWE-125.

After the guarded 16-byte probe accepts a candidate, this final scan walks from cname forward with no upper bound other than hitting a NUL or a non-printable byte. The inner j-loop only ever verified up to 16 bytes (and only while reloffset<descsz and noffset<size); if the accepted window is 16 fully-printable bytes with no NUL, this loop keeps dereferencing *cp past doff+prpsoffsets(i)+16, past descsz, and potentially past `size`, until it happens to find a stopping byte. That is an unbounded out-of-bounds read of the mapped image, exploitable for info-leak (the bytes are then printed by %.*s) or crash. Invariant: the walk must be clamped to min(size, doff+descsz). CWE-125; this is the documented donote note-parsing over-read family (CVE-2014-3710).

The 'try earlier offsets' refinement reads nbuf[no] across [doff+prpsoffsets(k), doff+prpsoffsets(i)) using only the prps offset table, with none of the noffset>=size / reloffset>=descsz guards that protected the primary probe. If prpsoffsets(i) is large relative to the actual descsz/size, this loop over-reads the buffer while computing `adjust`. Invariant: every nbuf[no] here must also be bounded by size (and descsz). CWE-125.

The note header itself is copied from nbuf[offset] with no local guard that offset + xnh_sizeof <= size. donote trusts the caller's note-walking loop (in doshn/dophn) to have validated that a full Elf32_Nhdr/Elf64_Nhdr fits before the call. If any caller path advances `offset` to within fewer than xnh_sizeof bytes of the end (e.g. via the aligned returns doff / ELF_ALIGN(doff+descsz) that this very function produces, or a truncated final note), this reads up to sizeof(Elf64_Nhdr) bytes past the buffer. Invariant: the caller MUST ensure offset + xnh_sizeof <= size for every invocation; this is a latent contract the header/implementation must uphold. CWE-125.

namesz/descsz are only rejected when the top bit is set, so each may be as large as 0x7FFFFFFF. On a 32-bit target (size_t is 32-bit) the subsequent `offset + namesz` (lines 40, 42) and `doff + descsz` (lines 49, 50) can integer-overflow/wrap, making the bounds checks `offset + namesz > size` and `doff + descsz > size` pass for a value that actually points far past the buffer; ELF_ALIGN(offset + namesz) can also wrap. The result is that doff is computed from wrapped arithmetic and every later nbuf[doff+...] access reads out of bounds. Invariant: reject namesz/descsz that exceed (size - offset) and detect the addition overflow, not merely the sign bit. CWE-190 (leading to CWE-125).

The many name matches use strcmp/strncmp on &nbuf[noff], which read the buffer until a terminating NUL rather than being limited to namesz. In practice the offset+namesz<=size guard bounds the name region and the comparison stops at the constant's own length ("GNU"+NUL = 4 bytes, etc.), so a mismatch or the constant's NUL halts the scan within the verified region — this is low-risk here. It is worth confirming, though, that every reachable name buffer is NUL-terminated within `size`; a name of exactly namesz printable bytes with no trailing NUL would let strcmp read one byte past the name region if the constant matched that far (e.g. the namesz>=11 NetBSD-CORE strncmp is length-capped and safe, but the plain strcmp cases rely on the compared constant terminating first).

summary: This is file(1)/libmagic donote() ELF-note parsing over a fully attacker-controlled ELF image. The vendor-version handlers are tightly gated on exact namesz/descsz and copy only in-bounds fixed-size descriptors, but the ELFCORE path reads at hardcoded sub-offsets (doff+0x7c, doff+0x08) and re-scans command-name strings (lines 401-404, 409-412) using bounds that do not clamp to descsz/size, giving several remote out-of-bounds reads (CWE-125). Additionally the length gate only rejects the sign bit, so on 32-bit builds offset+namesz / doff+descsz can overflow (CWE-190) and the initial header memcpy (line 14) has no local size guard.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: donote(struct magic_set *ms, void *vbuf, size_t offset, size_t size, int clazz, int swap, size_t align, int *flags)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; offset/size/flags carry the trust boundary and the header-fits contract (line-14 memcpy).
Fixed-size stack copies of the note header; safe destinations, class-selected by the xnh_* macros.
Derived offsets; their danger is captured under the arithmetic/bounds findings (lines 40-50), not as standalone anchors.
Attacker-controlled 32-bit length fields; drive all offset arithmetic and are only sign-bit-filtered.
Cast of the input buffer; not itself a bug, but the base for all indexed reads.
Reads the note header with no local offset+xnh_sizeof<=size guard (latent caller contract).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: xnh_sizeof / xnh_addr / xnh_namesz / xnh_descsz / xnh_type macros
reviewed_anchors.reason: Class-selecting accessors; correct by construction, but xnh_namesz/xnh_descsz feed the length findings.
Only the sign bit is rejected; values up to 0x7FFFFFFF pass and can overflow 32-bit size_t arithmetic.
Aligned addition of an attacker length; can wrap on 32-bit and rounds doff above the value checked on line 42.
Name bounds check that can be defeated by additive overflow (CWE-190 finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: offset = ELF_ALIGN(doff + descsz); if (doff + descsz > size) (lines 49-50)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Descriptor bounds check; guarantees only descsz bytes at doff, not the fixed sub-offset reads, and can also overflow.
Duplicate-suppression logic; no memory-safety contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: GNU NT_GNU_VERSION handler, desc[4], memcpy sizeof(desc) (lines 61-98)
reviewed_anchors.reason: descsz==16 exactly equals sizeof(desc) and doff+descsz<=size verified; copy in-bounds.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: GNU NT_GNU_BUILD_ID handler, desc[5], memcpy descsz (lines 100-111)
reviewed_anchors.reason: descsz constrained to 16/20 <= 20 = sizeof(desc); copy bounded. Note: no return here (falls through), but no OOB.
descsz==4 fixed, in-bounds copy; remainder is integer formatting.
descsz==4 fixed, in-bounds copy; only arithmetic follows.
Prints a constant; never reads the descriptor.
descsz==4 fixed, in-bounds copy of the descriptor.
Name comparisons scan to a NUL; low-risk here because the name region is size-bounded and constants self-terminate, but NUL-termination within size should be confirmed.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: core: os_style detection: CORE / FreeBSD / NetBSD-CORE (lines 275-287)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Sets os_style only to valid enum constants; NetBSD-CORE match is length-capped strncmp(...,11).
Indexed only when os_style != -1, and os_style is only ever a valid enum value; in-bounds table read.
State/duplicate-suppression flags; no memory contract.
Fixed sub-offset read not bounded against descsz/size; out-of-bounds read.
Fixed sub-offset read not bounded against descsz; out-of-bounds read.
These guards correctly bound the primary per-char read nbuf[noffset] on line 365.
Reads nbuf[no] using prps offsets without size/descsz bound; out-of-bounds read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: cname scan for (cp = cname; *cp && isprint(*cp); cp++) (lines 409-412)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unbounded forward scan of the command name; classic donote over-read.
OS-specific offset table applied to attacker-sized descriptors; the surrounding scans are the OOB vector.
Fall-through resume value; no contract of its own.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of NetBSD core PROCINFO program name at fixed offset doff+0x7c
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (fixed offset into variable-length, attacker-sized descriptor)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0033_cwe399.c (donote), with the note contents controlled by the input ELF file
findings.invariant: Before reading the program name at doff+0x7c the code must verify descsz >= 0x7c + 32 AND doff + 0x7c + 31 < size; the only prior guarantee (doff+descsz<=size) does not cover this sub-offset.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker builds an ELF core note with name "NetBSD-CORE" style so os_style==OS_STYLE_NETBSD and xnh_type==NT_NETBSD_CORE_PROCINFO, but sets descsz small (e.g. 4). doff+0x7c then points beyond the descriptor, and if the note is near the end of the mapped image, beyond `size`. %.31s reads up to 31 bytes (and, lacking an in-window NUL, potentially further) past the valid buffer, disclosing adjacent heap/mapped memory in the printed output or crashing file(1).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no descsz-vs-0x7c bounds check on the NT_NETBSD_CORE_PROCINFO path and that nbuf is a bounded buffer of length size (not NUL-guaranteed past descsz).

findings.title: Unbounded scan of PRPSINFO command name past the descriptor/buffer
findings.invariant: The forward scan must stop at min(size, doff+descsz) as well as at a NUL/non-printable byte; it must not dereference *cp beyond the mapped buffer.
findings.failure_mode: The primary 16-byte probe accepts a candidate offset if all 16 bytes are printable (no NUL required to fall through). The subsequent `for (cp = cname; *cp && isprint(*cp); cp++)` then keeps reading forward with no size limit, so a descriptor whose printable bytes run up to the buffer edge causes reads past doff+descsz and past `size` until a stray NUL/non-printable byte is hit; the walked range is then printed by %.*s, leaking out-of-bounds memory or crashing.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the final cname loop (and the isprint(nbuf[no]) refinement loop at lines 401-404) have no `< nbuf+size` / `reloffset < descsz` clamp.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of NetBSD core signal number at fixed offset doff+0x08
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (fixed offset beyond attacker-sized descriptor)
findings.invariant: Verify descsz >= 0x08 + sizeof(signo) (and doff+0x0c <= size) before copying the signal word.
findings.failure_mode: On the same PROCINFO path, a descsz < 12 lets doff+0x08 reference bytes beyond the descriptor; if the note abuts the end of the image the 4-byte memcpy reads past `size`. Impact is a smaller info-leak/crash than the name over-read but shares the same missing-descsz-check root cause.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no descsz>=0x0c guard precedes the signo memcpy.

findings.title: Attacker length fields only sign-bit-filtered; 32-bit offset arithmetic can overflow and defeat bounds checks
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow in size arithmetic leading to bypassed bounds check
findings.invariant: Reject namesz/descsz that exceed (size - offset)/(size - doff) and detect additive overflow, rather than only rejecting the top bit; on 32-bit size_t, offset+namesz and doff+descsz must not wrap.
findings.failure_mode: namesz/descsz may be up to 0x7FFFFFFF. On a 32-bit build (size_t == 32-bit), `offset + namesz` (line 42) or `doff + descsz` (line 50), and the ELF_ALIGN rounding, can wrap around, making `> size` compare false for a descriptor that actually starts far past the buffer. doff is then a wrapped value and every subsequent nbuf[doff+...] read is out of bounds. On 64-bit builds the addition does not wrap but the ~2 GiB cap is still weaker than a real (size-offset) bound.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm size_t width on target builds and whether any caller pre-clamps namesz/descsz against the remaining buffer before donote uses them in the additions.

findings.title: Note header copied from nbuf[offset] without a local offset+xnh_sizeof<=size guard
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (missing precondition check on caller-supplied offset)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0033_cwe399.c (donote); precondition enforced in the caller loop, implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every caller MUST ensure offset + xnh_sizeof <= size before invoking donote; ideally donote should also self-check because it returns aligned offsets (doff, ELF_ALIGN(doff+descsz)) that a caller may feed straight back in.
findings.failure_mode: donote reads sizeof(Elf32_Nhdr)/sizeof(Elf64_Nhdr) bytes at nbuf[offset] with no size check. If a caller path advances offset to within fewer than xnh_sizeof bytes of the buffer end (truncated final note, or an aligned return value near size), this reads past the buffer. This is a latent contract bug: correctness depends entirely on the un-shown caller.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the doshn/dophn note loop validates offset+xnh_sizeof<=size (and the alignment padding) before each donote call.

findings.title: Name comparisons use strcmp on nbuf[noff] and rely on in-buffer NUL termination
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (string comparison beyond length field)
findings.invariant: The name at noff must be NUL-terminated within [noff, noff+namesz) (and within size); comparisons should ideally be length-bounded (strncmp against namesz) rather than trusting a terminator.
findings.failure_mode: strcmp scans until a terminating NUL rather than being limited to namesz. In practice the offset+namesz<=size guard bounds the region and each compared constant self-terminates, so a mismatch stops the scan inside the verified bytes; risk is low. But a name of exactly namesz printable bytes with no trailing NUL could let a matching-prefix strcmp read one byte past the name region. The length-capped strncmp cases ("CORE",4 / "NetBSD-CORE",11) are safe.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the note name bytes are guaranteed NUL-terminated within size for every strcmp path, or convert the comparisons to length-bounded form.

audit_checklist: In the NT_NETBSD_CORE_PROCINFO path, add descsz >= 0x7c+32 and descsz >= 0x0c checks (and doff+off+len <= size) before reading nbuf[doff+0x7c] and nbuf[doff+0x08].
Clamp the PRPSINFO command-name scans (lines 401-404 nbuf[no], and lines 409-412 cp walk) to min(size, doff+descsz), not just to a NUL/non-printable byte.
Replace the sign-bit-only namesz/descsz filter with a real bound against the remaining buffer, and guard offset+namesz / doff+descsz / ELF_ALIGN(...) against 32-bit integer overflow.
Verify the caller (doshn/dophn) ensures offset + xnh_sizeof <= size before every donote() call, including after the aligned return values doff and ELF_ALIGN(doff+descsz).
Confirm ELF_ALIGN rounding of offset+namesz cannot push doff past size without being caught by the line-50 doff+descsz>size check on every path.
Confirm note name buffers are NUL-terminated within size for all strcmp() name matches, or switch to length-bounded comparisons.
Confirm prpsoffsets(i) values are always < the smallest plausible descsz, or that both scan loops re-check reloffset < descsz for every access.
